assumptions: The question is about consolidated financial statements, not separate or individual financial statements.
Once the intragroup dividend is recognised as a receivable/payable, the analysis focuses on a recognised intragroup monetary item rather than on a forecast transaction.
A foreign exchange issue exists only if the receivable/payable is denominated in a currency that creates exchange differences not fully eliminated on consolidation.
If only the foreign currency component is designated, that component must satisfy the IFRS 9 risk-component conditions.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9's default rule is that only exposures with parties external to the reporting entity can be designated as hedged items in consolidated financial statements.
approaches.practical_implication: Absent a specific IFRS 9 exception, an intragroup dividend receivable cannot be hedge-designated in consolidated accounts.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 excludes intragroup items from hedged-item designation in consolidated financial statements, except stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 makes a specific exception for the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item when exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The recognised dividend balance is a monetary receivable/payable between group entities
The group entities involved have different functional currencies
The foreign currency gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedged item is reliably measurable
If only the FX component is designated, it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable
There is formal designation and documentation at inception
The hedge effectiveness and hedge ratio requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: The FX component of a recognised intragroup dividend receivable may be documented in a hedge relationship only if this exception and the general hedge-accounting criteria are satisfied.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when the exposure is not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraphs 6.3.2, 6.3.7, 6.4.1 and B6.3.8-B6.3.11 add reliable measurement, risk-component and documentation/effectiveness requirements.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 separately permits a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction to be hedged for FX risk, but that route addresses the pre-recognition stage rather than an already recognised receivable.
conditions: The transaction is still forecast rather than recognised
It is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the transacting entity
The foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss
approaches.practical_implication: This route is relevant only before the intragroup dividend is booked as a receivable/payable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 and B6.3.5-B6.3.6 allow forecast intragroup FX hedges only if the forecast transaction is highly probable and affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of foreign currency risk arising from net investments in foreign operations and says it should not be applied by analogy to other hedge types.
approaches.practical_implication: A recognised intragroup dividend receivable should not be justified as a net investment hedge merely because it creates FX exposure.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7-8 confine the Interpretation to net investment hedges and prohibit analogy to other hedge-accounting situations.
Paragraphs 10-14 define the hedged risk and hedged item as foreign currency risk on net assets of a foreign operation, not an intragroup receivable.
